lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: He jests at scars that never felt a wound. Juliet appears above at a window.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But, soft! what light through yonder window breaks?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Who is already sick and pale with grief,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That thou her maid art far more fair than she:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Be not her maid, since she is envious;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Her vestal livery is but sick and green

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And none but fools do wear it; cast it off.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It is my lady, O, it is my love!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, that she knew she were!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She speaks, yet she says nothing: what of that?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Her eye discourses; I will answer it.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I am too bold, ’tis not to me she speaks:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Having some business, do entreat her eyes

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To twinkle in their spheres till they return.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What if her eyes were there, they in her head?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Would through the airy region stream so bright

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That birds would sing and think it were not night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: See, how she leans her cheek upon her hand!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, that I were a glove upon that hand,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That I might touch that cheek!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ay me!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She speaks:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, speak again, bright angel! for thou art

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As glorious to this night, being o’er my head,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As is a winged messenger of heaven

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Unto the white-upturned wondering eyes

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Of mortals that fall back to gaze on him

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: When he bestrides the lazy-pacing clouds

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And sails upon the bosom of the air.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Deny thy father and refuse thy name;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And I’ll no longer be a Capulet.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: ’Tis but thy name that is my enemy;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thou art thyself, though not a Montague.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What’s Montague? it is nor hand, nor foot,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Belonging to a man. O, be some other name!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What’s in a name? that which we call a rose

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By any other name would smell as sweet;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call’d,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Retain that dear perfection which he owes

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Without that title. Romeo, doff thy name,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And for that name which is no part of thee

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Take all myself.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I take thee at thy word:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Call me but love, and I’ll be new baptized;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Henceforth I never will be Romeo.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What man art thou that thus bescreen’d in night

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So stumblest on my counsel?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By a name

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I know not how to tell thee who I am:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Because it is an enemy to thee;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Had I it written, I would tear the word.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My ears have not yet drunk a hundred words

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Of that tongue’s utterance, yet I know the sound:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Art thou not Romeo and a Montague?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Neither, fair saint, if either thee dislike.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: How camest thou hither, tell me, and wherefore?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The orchard walls are high and hard to climb,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And the place death, considering who thou art,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If any of my kinsmen find thee here.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With love’s light wings did I o’er-perch these walls;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For stony limits cannot hold love out,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And what love can do that dares love attempt;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Therefore thy kinsmen are no let to me.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If they do see thee, they will murder thee.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Alack, there lies more peril in thine eye

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Than twenty of their swords: look thou but sweet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And I am proof against their enmity.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I would not for the world they saw thee here.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I have night’s cloak to hide me from their sight;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And but thou love me, let them find me here:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My life were better ended by their hate,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Than death prorogued, wanting of thy love.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By whose direction found’st thou out this place?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By love, who first did prompt me to inquire;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: He lent me counsel and I lent him eyes.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I am no pilot; yet, wert thou as far

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As that vast shore wash’d with the farthest sea,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I would adventure for such merchandise.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thou know’st the mask of night is on my face,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Else would a maiden blush bepaint my cheek

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For that which thou hast heard me speak to-night.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Fain would I dwell on form, fain, fain deny

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What I have spoke: but farewell compliment!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Dost thou love me? I know thou wilt say “Ay,”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And I will take thy word: yet, if thou swear’st,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thou mayst prove false; at lovers’ perjuries,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: They say, Jove laughs. O gentle Romeo,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If thou dost love, pronounce it faithfully:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or if thou think’st I am too quickly won,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I’ll frown and be perverse and say thee nay,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So thou wilt woo; but else, not for the world.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In truth, fair Montague, I am too fond,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And therefore thou mayst think my ’havior light;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But trust me, gentleman, I’ll prove more true

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Than those that have more cunning to be strange.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I should have been more strange, I must confess,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But that thou overheard’st, ere I was ware,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My true love’s passion: therefore pardon me,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And not impute this yielding to light love,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which the dark night hath so discovered.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Lady, by yonder blessed moon I swear

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That tips with silver all these fruit-tree tops—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, swear not by the moon, the inconstant moon,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That monthly changes in her circled orb,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Lest that thy love prove likewise variable.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What shall I swear by?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Do not swear at all;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or, if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which is the god of my idolatry,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And I’ll believe thee.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: If my heart’s dear love—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Well, do not swear: although I joy in thee,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I have no joy of this contract to-night:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It is too rash, too unadvised, too sudden;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Too like the lightning, which doth cease to be

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ere one can say “It lightens.” Sweet, good night!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: This bud of love, by summer’s ripening breath,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: May prove a beauteous flower when next we meet.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Good night, good night! as sweet repose and rest

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come to thy heart as that within my breast!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, wilt thou leave me so unsatisfied?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What satisfaction canst thou have to-night?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The exchange of thy love’s faithful vow for mine.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I gave thee mine before thou didst request it:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And yet I would it were to give again.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Wouldst thou withdraw it? for what purpose, love?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But to be frank, and give it thee again.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And yet I wish but for the thing I have:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My bounty is as boundless as the sea,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My love as deep; the more I give to thee,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The more I have, for both are infinite. Nurse calls within.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I hear some noise within; dear love, adieu!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Anon, good nurse! Sweet Montague, be true.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Stay but a little, I will come again.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O blessed, blessed night! I am afeard,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being in night, all this is but a dream,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Too flattering-sweet to be substantial.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Three words, dear Romeo, and good night indeed.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If that thy bent of love be honourable,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thy purpose marriage, send me word to-morrow,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By one that I’ll procure to come to thee,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where and what time thou wilt perform the rite;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And all my fortunes at thy foot I’ll lay

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And follow thee my lord throughout the world.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Madam!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I come, anon.—But if thou mean’st not well,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I do beseech thee—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Madam!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By and by, I come:—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To cease thy suit, and leave me to my grief:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To-morrow will I send.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: So thrive my soul—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: A thousand times good night!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A thousand times the worse, to want thy light.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Love goes toward love, as schoolboys from their books,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But love from love, toward school with heavy looks. Retiring.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hist! Romeo, hist! O, for a falconer’s voice,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To lure this tassel-gentle back again!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Bondage is hoarse, and may not speak aloud;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Else would I tear the cave where Echo lies,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And make her airy tongue more hoarse than mine,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: With repetition of my Romeo’s name.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It is my soul that calls upon my name:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: How silver-sweet sound lovers’ tongues by night,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Like softest music to attending ears!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Romeo!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My dear?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: At what o’clock to-morrow

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall I send to thee?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: At the hour of nine.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I will not fail: ’tis twenty years till then.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I have forgot why I did call thee back.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Let me stand here till thou remember it.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I shall forget, to have thee still stand there,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Remembering how I love thy company.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And I’ll still stay, to have thee still forget,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Forgetting any other home but this.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: ’Tis almost morning; I would have thee gone:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And yet no further than a wanton’s bird;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Who lets it hop a little from her hand,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Like a poor prisoner in his twisted gyves,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And with a silk thread plucks it back again,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So loving-jealous of his liberty.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I would I were thy bird.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Sweet, so would I:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Yet I should kill thee with much cherishing.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Good night, good night! parting is such sweet sorrow,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That I shall say good night till it be morrow.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Sleep dwell upon thine eyes, peace in thy breast!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Would I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hence will I to my ghostly father’s cell,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: His help to crave, and my dear hap to tell.

glosses.phrase: He jests at scars that never felt a wound
glosses.definition: Mercutio mocks wounds because he has never truly loved — proverb-like dismissal
but wait; hush
jealous moon — the moon is personified as Juliet's romantic rival
Juliet as a vestal virgin attendant of Diana (the moon)
virgin's uniform — the green-white of Diana's priestesses
the pale-green color of chlorosis, the 'green-sickness' of lovesick virgins
his beloved — courtly-love address
her eye speaks
revolve in their celestial orbits — Ptolemaic cosmology
outshine the stars
the upper atmosphere
Juliet's sigh — the 'ay me' Mercutio mocked is now really spoken
Romeo addresses Juliet as a celestial being
an angel; a divine herald
rides astride the slow-moving clouds
WHY are you named Romeo (not 'where are you'); the single most misread line in Shakespeare — 'wherefore' means 'why,' NEVER 'where'
WHY; for what reason — NOT 'where.' Juliet is asking why Romeo has to be a Montague, not asking where he is

glosses.phrase: Deny thy father and refuse thy name
glosses.definition: disown your family; reject your surname
she'd give up her name for his
it's only his name, 'Montague,' that is her enemy — not him
what does a name matter? — the play's most famous rhetorical question on language and essence

glosses.phrase: By any other name would smell as sweet
glosses.definition: a rose's essence is independent of its label — Juliet's philosophical claim
take off; lay aside
hidden; screened
private thoughts
Romeo resumes the pilgrim/saint language of 1.5
fly over; leap across
the garden walls of stone
invulnerable to their hostility — love as armor

glosses.phrase: Fain would I dwell on form
glosses.definition: I would willingly maintain propriety — Juliet on why she can't play the coy beloved now
courtly etiquette
sworn lies
takes lightly
swear your love sincerely
wanton; promiscuous affection — Juliet disclaims frivolity
Romeo's proposed witness — Juliet rejects swearing by the moon
the moon is changeable (waxing/waning) — a bad oath-object
the worship of false gods — Juliet's half-joking caveat
betrothal; formal exchange of promises
rash; without deliberation
too sudden, too brief — Juliet's most self-aware warning
generosity; abundance

glosses.phrase: My bounty is as boundless as the sea
glosses.definition: Juliet's love transcends limit — one of the play's loveliest lines
a soft call to attention; 'hey'
she wishes for a hunter's call that trains hawks to return
male peregrine falcon — a noble bird, here Romeo
the nymph who pined away for Narcissus, leaving only her voice
Juliet's recognition of Romeo's voice in the orchard dark
Juliet sets the Nurse's appointment — the fateful next-morning meeting
a lovers' game of pretending forgetfulness to prolong parting
a pet bird held by a careless girl who won't let it fly
fetters; leg-chains
good fortune
the oxymoron — Juliet's paradoxical farewell, one of the most quoted lines
from here; away
spiritual — pertaining to the soul
Friar Laurence's chamber — 'ghostly' meant 'spiritual,' not ghost-like
fortunate event

annotations.title: 'What light through yonder window breaks?' — Juliet as the sun
annotations.body: Romeo's opening speech in the orchard transforms Juliet into a cosmic event: she is the sun rising in the east, brighter than stars, outshining day. The imagery is Petrarchan in its extravagance but new in its direction — Rosaline was cataloged; Juliet is made weather, is made sky. Notice the envious moon: for Romeo, the moon is Diana (chastity, the virgin goddess), and Juliet is the sun who will eclipse her. Urging Juliet to 'arise' and 'kill the envious moon' is urging her to abandon her virginity — the whole speech is erotic argument disguised as cosmology. This is Romeo's first speech in the new voice: the Rosaline-Petrarchism of 1.1 has been replaced with a vocabulary that is hotter, more physical, more solar.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

annotations.title: 'Wherefore art thou Romeo?' — why, not where
annotations.body: The single most misread line in all of Shakespeare. Juliet is not asking where Romeo is hiding; he is not yet visible to her, but she isn't calling out to locate him. 'Wherefore' in Shakespeare's English means 'why' (from Old English 'hwær-for,' 'for what cause'). Juliet is asking the cosmos a question: why do you have to be a Montague? Her next lines make the point explicit: 'Deny thy father and refuse thy name.' The whole speech pivots on the arbitrariness of names versus the substance of being — 'What's in a name? that which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet.' This is the play's linguistic-philosophical climax: Juliet proposes that identity is separable from its label, that Romeo is 'himself' though he is not 'a Montague.' The tragedy will prove that the world does not agree.

annotations.title: Juliet on the contract — the play's most self-aware voice
annotations.body: In the middle of the balcony-scene's ecstasy, Juliet stops to name the danger: 'I have no joy of this contract to-night: / It is too rash, too unadvised, too sudden; / Too like the lightning, which doth cease to be / Ere one can say it lightens.' These are astonishing lines for the play's thirteen-year-old heroine to speak — a self-critique of her own love as it happens. The three-adjective compression ('too rash, too unadvised, too sudden') resembles the Friar's later 'these violent delights have violent ends.' Juliet, like the Friar, sees the tragedy's shape; unlike him, she proceeds anyway, but with open eyes. The scene earns its sweetness because it has bought it by acknowledging its cost.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

annotations.title: 'Parting is such sweet sorrow' — the closing oxymoron
annotations.body: Juliet's farewell to Romeo condenses the whole play into five words: parting is sweet sorrow. The oxymoron recalls Romeo's Petrarchan cold-fire in 1.1 ('feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health') — but there, the paradoxes were stacked for performance. Here, the paradox is earned. Parting is sweet because the prospect of meeting again is sweeter; parting is sorrow because the meeting is uncertain. Juliet has learned to handle the same rhetorical figure Romeo flaunted, but with a difference: her oxymoron describes what she actually feels. This is also the scene's formal lesson — love discovers its own language, different from the clichés it inherits.

trials.prompt: In 'O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo?' — what does 'wherefore' mean?
options: Where are you hiding
WHY are you named Romeo — i.e., why must you be a Montague (NOT 'where are you')
In what manner are you acting
Since when are you Romeo

trials.prompt: What philosophical claim does Juliet make in 'What's in a name? that which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet'?
options: Names are deeply tied to identity
A thing's essence is separable from its label — identity is not fixed by the name society assigns
Roses are the only true flowers
Latin names are more reliable than English

trials.prompt: What controlling image shapes Romeo's opening speech in the orchard?
options: Juliet as a flower
Juliet as the sun rising in the east, outshining the envious moon
Juliet as a ship in a storm
Juliet as a caged bird

trials.prompt: Juliet says the contract is 'too rash, too unadvised, too sudden.' What does this tell us about her?
options: She is rejecting Romeo
She is the play's most self-aware voice — she sees the danger of her own love and proceeds with open eyes
She has been coached by Friar Laurence
She is nervous about her father

trials.prompt: 'Parting is such sweet sorrow' is an example of —
options: Metonymy
Oxymoron (two contradictory terms yoked together)
Anaphora
Synecdoche

trials.prompt: Why does Juliet refuse Romeo's oath 'by the moon'?
options: She thinks Romeo is joking
The moon is inconstant — it waxes and wanes — and would make an unreliable witness
Swearing by the moon is blasphemous
She cannot see the moon from the balcony
